name: Sony BRAVIA 9 65" Mini LED QLED 4K Smart TV
tag: Best Mini-LED TV for Dark Room Precision

specs.Gaming: 2x HDMI 2.1 (4K/120Hz), VRR, Auto Low Latency Mode
specs.Smart Platform: Google TV (Bravia Core, PlayStation content hub)

whatWorks: Local dimming precision is the best in its class — Sony's XR Backlight Master Drive produces OLED-like contrast in dark scenes with virtually no visible blooming, something competing Mini-LED TVs at $1,500 less still struggle with
The XR Cognitive Processor delivers the most natural color and motion processing available in a non-OLED TV — skin tones, sports motion, and film grain look genuinely cinematic rather than processed
Anti-reflective screen coating is exceptional for a Mini-LED TV — handles ambient light better than most OLEDs at a similar price while maintaining color saturation
Google TV integration with native PlayStation content hubs and Bravia Core streaming makes it the clear pick for PS5 owners who want deep ecosystem integration
worthKnowing: Only 2 HDMI 2.1 ports — PS5 + Xbox Series X owners will need a switch or sacrifice one connection, which is a frustrating limitation at this price point
No HDR10+ support — Sony uses only Dolby Vision and HDR10. HDR10+ titles on Amazon Prime Video and some Blu-rays won't display at their best
At $3,198 CAD, it's a significant premium over the Hisense U88QG ($1,398) for incremental gains in local dimming precision. The value gap is real and worth being honest about
Gaming input lag in 4K/120Hz is good but not class-leading — dedicated gamers who want the absolute lowest input lag should look at the Hisense U88QG or LG C5

commonComplaints.title: Only 2 HDMI 2.1 ports on a $3,000+ TV frustrates gamers
commonComplaints.body: Only 2 HDMI 2.1 ports is a consistent owner complaint — at $3,000+ CAD, most buyers expect 4 HDMI 2.1 ports. Connecting a PS5, Xbox, and PC simultaneously requires a switch.

commonComplaints.title: No HDR10+ support — Amazon Prime Video HDR content unaffected
commonComplaints.body: No HDR10+ is a frustrating omission that Sony refuses to address — Amazon Prime Video HDR content and certain Blu-ray discs cap at HDR10 quality instead of the enhanced HDR10+ grade.

commonComplaints.title: Price-to-performance gap vs. Hisense U88QG questioned in r/hometheater
commonComplaints.body: Price-to-performance gap vs. Hisense U88QG is a recurring theme in r/hometheater — many buyers who auditioned both say the Bravia 9's improvements don't justify a $1,800 CAD premium for most living-room use cases.

verdict: The Sony Bravia 9 is the best Mini-LED TV for buyers who prioritize dark-scene precision and don't want to accept OLED burn-in risk. Sony's local dimming is genuinely class-leading — closer to OLED than any other LCD technology available. The trade-offs are real: only 2 HDMI 2.1 ports, no HDR10+, and a $3,198 CAD price tag that's hard to justify unless picture quality is the absolute priority. For most buyers, the Hisense U88QG delivers 80% of the performance at 44% of the price.

realBuyers.quote: Watched The Batman in 4K and the dark scenes looked as good as my friend's LG C2 — genuinely surprised. The blooming I expected just isn't there.
Would have been perfect with 4 HDMI 2.1 ports. Having to disconnect my PC every time I want to plug in the Switch is annoying for a $3,000 TV.
Sony's motion processing is the reason I bought this over the Hisense. Sports and live TV look miles better — less of that soap opera effect even when you want smooth motion.

sources.title: AVForums: Sony BRAVIA 9 (65XR90) Mini LED TV Review

metaDescription: Sony Bravia 9 65" Mini-LED review for Canada. Best-in-class local dimming, Dolby Vision, $3,198 CAD. Honest take on HDMI limitations, no HDR10+, and who should buy it.
introText: The Sony Bravia 9 is Sony's 2024 flagship Mini-LED TV and the strongest argument yet that LCD can challenge OLED on picture quality. Its XR Backlight Master Drive local dimming is the most precise available in any non-OLED panel — dark scenes show virtually no blooming. The trade-offs are real: two HDMI 2.1 ports, no HDR10+, and a $3,198 CAD price that demands serious justification.
